coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of fluorescent silicon quantum dots for ratiometric sensing of Hg (II) based on the regulation of energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenjia
Liu, Dong
Dong, Daming
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: The rapid and sensitive detection of Hg2+ is highly required to protect the environmental safety and human healthy. In the present work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform, consisting of silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), Rox-labelled DNA (Rox-DNA), and Exonuclease III (Exo III), is developed for the accurate detection of Hg2+. As for fluorescent probe, we report the first use of glutathione as reduction reagent for the microwave synthesis of SiQDs, achieving the facile (using a house-hold microwave oven) and rapid (within 8 min) synthesis. Such SiQDs show pH-independent spectra and reversible fluorescent behavior with temperature. Moreover, experimental results revealed that the electrostatic interaction-induced aggregation of Rox-DNA and SiQDs facilitated the occurring of energy transfer (ET). And detection principle based on the regulation of ET between Rox and SiQDs with Exo III was designed for analysis. ET effect resulted in the fluorescent fading of Rox while that of SiQDs kept stable. For analysis, the addition of Hg2+ led to the formation of double-stranded Rox-DNA via T-Hg2+-T. Exo III would cut these double-stranded DNA to release Rox and Hg2+, thereby impeding the ET effect and recovering the fluorescent of Rox. Such SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform exhibited a linear response concentration range of 0.02 nM–10 nM with a detection limit of 0.01 nM. It was successfully used to analyze the water and soil samples. The reliability was validated by ICP-MS. Our work should promote the practical application of ratiometric fluorescent assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent assay
Silicon quantum dots
Microwave-assisted synthesis
Exonuclease III
Mercury (II) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ ions in water and soil samples
SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III fluorescent sensing of Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ ions in samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61901193 , 22074055 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20200104 ), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province , Project of Faculty of Agricultural Equipment of Jiangsu University , Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescent emission spectra of DAMO, GSH and the synthesized SiQDs with DAMO and GSH (DAMO + GSH (SiQDs)). (B) Images of products prepared with different reaction times. Effects of (C) molar ratio of GSH to DAMO (CGSH/CDAMO) and (D) reaction time on fluorescent intensity.

(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra of SiQDs. Inset of A is the photograph of SiQDs under ambient and UV light (365 nm). (B) Emission spectra of SiQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (C) TEM image of SiQDs. Inset of C refers to the corresponding (a) size distribution and (b) HRTEM. (D) FT-IR spectra of SiQDs. (E) Survey XPS spectra and (F) high-resolution Si 2p of SiQDs.

(A) Time-scan spectrum of SiQDs under 350 nm excitation. (B) Storage stability of SiQDs. Fluorescent behaviors of SiQDs at (C) different concentrations of NaCl, (D) pH values and (E) temperatures. (F) Reversible fluorescent behavior with temperature of SiQDs.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of SiQDs, Rox-DNA, SiQDs + Hg2+, SiQDs + Rox-DNA, SiQDs + Rox-DNA + Hg2+ and SiQDs + Rox-DNA + Hg2++Exo III. (B) Zeta potential of SiQDs, Rox-DNA, and their mixture. (C) fluorescent lifetimes of SiQDs and (D) Rox in different systems.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III platform in the presence of various CHg 2+. (B) The plot of I607/I414 versus CHg 2+. (C) The dependence of I607/I414 on the logarithm of CHg 2+. (D) Selectivity to 0.5 nM Hg2+ over other ions (50 nM).
Schematic illustration of (A) microwave-assisted synthesis of SiQDs and (B) SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III fluorescent sensing system for Hg2+ analysis.
Comparisons of fluorescent assays for Hg2+ analysis.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: F, fluorescent; RF, ratiometric fluorescent; FAM, carboxyfluorescein; GO, graphene oxide; Exo III, exonuclease III; SG, SYBR Green I; NCs, nanoclusters; NPs, nanoparticles; PDNTs, polydopamine nanotubes; PCDs, polymer carbon dots; TPPS, tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid hydrate; DTT, dithiothreitol; LACC, laccase; OPD, o-phenylenediamine; Rox, 6-carboxyX-rhodamine; ND, nanodot; QDs, quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of fluorescent silicon quantum dots for ratiometric sensing of Hg (II) based on the regulation of energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Intelligent Equipment for Agriculture, Beijing Academy of Agriculture and Forestry Sciences, Beijing, 100097, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Intelligent Equipment for Agriculture
Beijing Academy of Agriculture and Forestry Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center of Intelligent Equipment for Agriculture, Beijing Academy of Agriculture and Forestry Sciences, Beijing 100097, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid and sensitive detection of Hg2+ is highly required to protect the environmental safety and human healthy. In the present work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform, consisting of silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), Rox-labelled DNA (Rox-DNA), and Exonuclease III (Exo III), is developed for the accurate detection of Hg2+. As for fluorescent probe, we report the first use of glutathione as reduction reagent for the microwave synthesis of SiQDs, achieving the facile (using a house-hold microwave oven) and rapid (within 8 min) synthesis. Such SiQDs show pH-independent spectra and reversible fluorescent behavior with temperature. Moreover, experimental results revealed that the electrostatic interaction-induced aggregation of Rox-DNA and SiQDs facilitated the occurring of energy transfer (ET). And detection principle based on the regulation of ET between Rox and SiQDs with Exo III was designed for analysis. ET effect resulted in the fluorescent fading of Rox while that of SiQDs kept stable. For analysis, the addition of Hg2+ led to the formation of double-stranded Rox-DNA via T-Hg2+-T. Exo III would cut these double-stranded DNA to release Rox and Hg2+, thereby impeding the ET effect and recovering the fluorescent of Rox. Such SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform exhibited a linear response concentration range of 0.02 nM–10 nM with a detection limit of 0.01 nM. It was successfully used to analyze the water and soil samples. The reliability was validated by ICP-MS. Our work should promote the practical application of ratiometric fluorescent assay.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercuric ion (Hg2+) accumulation could seriously threaten the environmental safety and further the human health with central nervous systems damage, which has already gained the global concern [1]. The rapid and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ is urgently required. Analytical techniques based on sophisticated instruments such as inductively coupled plasma-mass and cold-vapor atomic absorption spectrometry, however, are generally applied to the laboratory analysis [2,3]. In this regard, many fluorescent-based assays have been proposed to achieve the rapid detection of Hg2+ in various samples [4]. Yet, high accuracy and reliability are still the common challenges for fluorescent detection of trace Hg2+. Typically, there are two key factors affecting the fluorescent analysis, i.e., 1) sensing strategy related to the response signal generation and processing, and 2) fluorophore that serves to output signals.

Ratiometry, an efficient strategy to acquire superior analytical properties (e.g., better reliability, higher accuracy), has been extensively explored in fluorescent assays [5,6]. The detection principle relies on the utilization of the ratio of two independent fluorescent emissions as response signal to eliminate the influences caused by the fluctuations of excitation sources, sample-induced light scattering, or the environmental variety [7]. Currently, ratiometric fluorescent sensors are mainly composed of two fluorophores or their hybrid such as carbon dot and gold nanocluster (AuNC), UCNP@SiO2@Cy5-pep, and silicon particle (SiNP)/AuNC nanohybrid [8–10]. In these reports, detections were mainly achieved based on the direct interaction between target analyte and fluorophore. Yet, the lack of excellent selectivity is a vital limitation, particularly for the analysis of complex systems. For example, to eliminate the influence of Ag+ on Hg2+ analysis in the SiNP/AuNC-based sensing system, extra anti-interference procedure of adding NaCl was needed [10]. Then the investigation on ratiometric fluorescent aptasensing emerges to be the focus due to the attracting selectivity by specifically chelating with thymine nucleotide (T) to form T-Hg2+-T [11]. And interactions between different fluorophores such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) are exploited to construct aptasensors for Hg2+ analysis [12]. The opposite fluorescent responses are generally obtained in systems such as AuNC and 2,3-diaminophenazine, CdTe/CdS QDs and Rhodamine 6G, which often showed enhanced sensitivity, selectivity and reliability [13,14]. More recently, He et al. described a ratiometric assay based on silicon nanodot (SiND) and 6-carboxy-X-rhodamine (Rox)-labelled DNA, in which only the fluorescent of Rox changed upon the presence of Hg2+ [15]. The variations in fluorescent originated from the distance-sensitive energy transfer (ET) between SiND and Rox, which was regulated by forming T-Hg2+-T. As compared with that of IFE or FRET, ET would only quench the fluorescent of donor fluorophore while that of acceptor keeps constant [16,17]. There are limited reports on the investigation of ET to detect Hg2+, and exploring ET-based ratiometric fluorescent aptasensing strategy may be a good choice.

Fluorophore plays a vital role in the sensing, and QDs such as CdTe and CdSe QDs are among the ideal fluorophores due to symmetric and narrow emission spectra and good photostability [18]. The toxicity of such QDs, however, seriously limits their sensing applications, whereas the environment-friendly SiQDs with comparable optical properties are promising alternatives. And the sophisticated synthesis procedures and hydrophobic surface of SiQDs are the major challenges [19,20]. The former decreases the reproducibility of different batches, while the latter makes pre-treatment essential for applications. Therefore, the facile preparation of hydrophilic SiQDs is of extreme importance. In this respect, He's group has made some pioneering works. In 2013, they reported a microwave-assisted strategy to prepare hydrophilic SiQDs within 15 min using (3-aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane as silicon source and trisodium citrate dihydrate as reducing agent [21]. From then on, many works are focused on this field, and several silicon sources (e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO)) and reductants (e.g., sodium ascorbate, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt) are utilized to prepare SiQDs [22,23]. In these reports, specialized instruments are essential to offer the microwave irradiation. Developing more convenient routes to synthesize SiQDs with excellent optical properties is still one of the focal points.

Herein, we proposed a convenient ET-based ratiometric fluorescent assay to achieve the accurate and reliable analysis of Hg2+. SiQDs was synthesized with a household microwave oven within 8 min. The sensing system consisted of SiQDs, Rox-labelled DNA, and exonuclease III (Exo III). The aggregation of Rox-DNA and SiQDs induced by electrostatic interaction favored the occurring of ET. The addition of Hg2+ led to the formation of double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) via T-Hg2+-T. Exo III would cut these dsDNA to release Rox and Hg2+, thereby inhibiting ET and restoring the fluorescent of Rox. Thus, fluorescent “turn-on” Rox was observed while that of SiQDs kept constant, while their intensity ratio can be used to estimate Hg2+ concentration.

N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO, 95%, Aladdin), l-glutathione reduced (GSH, ≥98%, Sigma-Aldrich), and mercury nitrate monohydrate (Hg(NO3)2·H2O, SCRC) were employed in this experiment. Exonuclease III (Exo III) and Rox-labelled oligonucleotides (5′-Rox-TTTGTTTCGTTCGTTCGTTTCTTT-3′, Rox-DNA) were supplied by Sangon Biotechnology. 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.0, containing 10 mM MgCl2) was used for the experiment. Millipore-Q water was used to prepare solutions.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were collected using a UV-3600 plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescent spectra were obtained using Edinburgh FS5 (UK) spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained via a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). A zetasizer instrument (Malvern Nano-ZS Zeta Sizer, UK) was used to record the zeta potentials. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analysis was performed on a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was operated with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded on a BRUCKER D8 X-ray diffractometer operated at 40 kV. An ICAP Qc ICP-MS (Thermo Scientific) device was used for the real sample analysis.

The procedure for the synthesis of SiQDs was as follows: 0.2 mL DAMO was added into 20 mL nitrogen-saturated aqueous solution containing 20 mM L-GSH. After 10 min nitrogen bubbling, the mixture reacted for 8 min using a household microwave oven (700 W). The resultant brownish yellow solid was dispersed in ultrapure water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm. Then the supernatant was dialyzed and freeze-dried, and the obtained SiQDs powder was stored at 4 °C before use.

A mixture of 50 μL 0.5 mg/mL SiQDs, 50 μL 1 μM Rox-DNA and 265 μL Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0, containing 10 mM MgCl2) was incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. Then, 20 μL Hg2+ standard solution with certain concentration was added and incubated for 40 min. After that, 15 μL 2 U/μL Exo III was added into the mixture to incubate 65 min. At last, the solution was heated to 80 °C to end the reaction and then cooled to room temperature. The fluorescent intensities of the resultant solution at 414 and 607 nm were collected using the excitation wavelength of 350 and 550 nm, respectively. Without special statement, the slits for all the fluorescent measurements were 3 nm.
The selectivity experiment was carried out by detecting 0.5 nM Hg2+ at the presence of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, and Cd2+ with concentrations of 50 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ ions in water and soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: Both ancient canal water and farmland soil samples were collected locally in Zhenjiang (China, Jiangsu). The water sample was filtered with a 0.45 μm membrane before use. The soil sample was pretreated according to official method GBT22105.1–2008. Briefly, 0.5 g soil sample was soaked with aqua regia (10 mL), and then heated with refluxing for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged, and the pH of the supernatant was adjusted to 7.0 with 1 M NaOH. Then, the solution was diluted to a volume of 50 mL with ultrapure water. The standard addition method was used to validate the applicability of the proposed sensing platform. The real sample analysis using ICP-MS was entrusted to Beijing ZHONG KE BAI CE Technology Service Co., Ltd.

The present research aims to develop a rapid and efficient ratiometric fluorescent assay to realize the accurate and reliable analysis of Hg2+. Herein, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), the fluorophore, is prepared via a microwave-assisted synthetic route using N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) and glutathione (GSH) (Scheme 1 A). The proposed ratiometric fluorescent aptasensing system is comprised of three components, i.e., SiQDs, Rox-labelled DNA (Rox-DNA), and Exonuclease III (Exo III) (Scheme 1B). The working principle is based on the regulation of energy transfer (ET) between Rox and SiQDs by using Exo III. Briefly, Rox acts as the donor while SiQDs serves as the receptor, and ET effect only leads to the fluorescent fading of Rox (I607) while that of SiQDs (I414) keeps stable. To achieve the better quenching efficiency, the aggregation of Rox-DNA and SiQDs is designed on the basis of electrostatic interaction to strengthen the effect of ET. For analysis, the addition of Hg2+ leads to the formation of double-stranded Rox-DNA via T-Hg2+-T. And Exo III cut these double-stranded DNA to release Rox and Hg2+, and impedes ET effect and restoring the fluorescent of Rox. In theory, such a process would cycle until the total enzymolysis of Rox-DNA by Exo III, offering the significantly amplified response signal. In this way, the “turn-on” fluorescent of Rox is observed while that of SiQDs remains constant, and the ratio of their fluorescent intensities, I607/I414, can be used to estimate the Hg2+ concentration.

The microwave-assisted route has already exhibited high efficiency in the synthesis of SiQDs using DAMO or (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane [21,22]. Herein, to achieve the rapid and convenient synthesis, DAMO and GSH were selected as the silicon source and reductant, respectively, to prepare SiQDs using a household microwave furnace (700 W). Fig. 1 A shows that only the simultaneous presence of DAMO and GSH in the reaction system generates the obvious fluorescent response, indicating the formation of SiQDs. Based on previous reports, Scheme 1A illustrates the possible formation mechanism of SiQDs [21,24]. Briefly, the reduction of DAMO by GSH occurs under the microwave irradiation and further generates crystal nuclei in Step 1. Subsequently, nanocrystals with small sizes via Ostwald ripening forms (Step 2). As the growth continues, large-sized SiQDs with enhanced stability generated by dissolving and absorbing small nanocrystals (Step 3). In this way, SiQDs can be conveniently prepared. Noteworthy, this synthesis experiment was performed in an open reactor. The color of the product becomes darker as the reaction time extends, and brownish yellow solid was eventually obtained at 8 min (Fig. 1B).

To acquire stronger fluorescent intensity, the parameters for synthesis including the reactant ratio of GSH and DAMO (CGSH/CDAMO) and reaction time were optimized. Fig. 1C reveals that the intensity increases as the rising of CGSH/CDAMO until reaches 0.4. The explanation is that the large amount of GSH may lead to the generation of excessive crystal nuclei and further the insufficiency of DAMO to form large-sized nanocrystals. Then, CGSH/CDAMO was selected to be 0.4. As shown in Fig. 1D, as the reaction time extends, the fluorescent intensity increases and then decreases after 8 min. It is ascribed to that the microwave irradiation may destroy the structure of SiQDs after the evaporation of liquid in the reaction system. Thus, 8 min was the optimal reaction time.

The morphology and optical properties of the resultant SiQDs were characterized. Inset of Fig. 2 A displays that the aqueous solution of SiQDs is transparent under natural light, but exhibits the strong blue luminescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The absorption peak locates at 312 nm, and the maximum excitation and maximum emission are at 350 nm and 414 nm, respectively (Fig. 2A). The fluorescent lifetime and quantum yield of SiQDs are 5.21 ns and 0.3%, respectively, showing relatively poor performances as compared with some previous reports [25]. Meanwhile, emission wavelength varies as the excitation wavelength changes, suggesting the inhomogeneous surface of SiQDs (Fig. 2B). TEM image displays uniform distribution of SiQDs with an average diameter of 2.6 nm (Fig. 2C). Nevertheless, lattice fringe can be hardly observed in the HRTEM image, indicating the amorphous structure of SiQDs (inset of Fig. 2C). And the XRD measurement further confirms this result (Fig. S1A).

Subsequently, FT-IR and XPS were carried out to analyze the surface and element composition of SiQDs. Based on FT-IR analysis, absorbance peaks at 1673 and 3389 cm−1 are assigned to N–H bond, while that at 2931 cm−1 corresponds to O–H bond (Fig. 2D). Both suggest the abundant hydrophilic amino groups on the surface of SiQDs, which favors the excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution in Fig. 2A. The two peaks at 1032 and 1116 cm−1 in the spectrum originates from the vibrational stretch of Si–O–H and Si–O–Si bonding [26]. And the bending vibration of C–O is observed at 1398 cm−1. According to XPS spectrum, the peaks at the binding energy values of 101.92, 153.26, 285.00, 399.33 and 531.48 eV in Fig. 2E are assigned to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. And the corresponding atom ratios are calculated to be 7.95, 54.97, 15.30 and 21.79% for Si, C, N and O element, respectively. High-resolution Si 2p analysis reveals the presence of Si–C (100.80 eV), Si–N (101.43 eV), and Si–O (102.10 eV) (Fig. 2F) [27]; four different types of C atoms, including C–Si (284.00 eV), C–C (284.80 eV), C–N/C–O (285.66 eV), and CO (287.40 eV), are confirmed according to the analysis of C1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) [28]. Meanwhile, the formation of Si–N (398.48 eV) and C–N–C (399.10 eV) are validated by N 1s spectrum, and O–Si (530.40, 531.70 eV) and CO (531.00 eV) are observed in O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1C and S1D) [29–31]. The results of FT-IR and XPS analysis are in good agreement, implying the successfully synthesis of SiQDs functionalized with abundant hydrophilic groups.

The stability of fluorophore under the conditions of long-term irradiation or storage, mediums with various ionic strengths or pH, and high temperature remains challenging. To acquire a more comprehensive understanding, effects of these factors on fluorescent properties of SiQDs were discussed in this work.

The long-term stability after irradiation and storage was tested. As shown in Fig. 3 A, after 7000 s of irradiation, the emission intensity of SiQDs keeps 93.6% of initial value, indicating the high resistance to photobleaching. Meanwhile, after stored at 4 °C for 30 days, the resultant SiQDs maintain 92.1% of the initial intensity, showing the robust storage stability (Fig. 3B).

The tolerance of SiQDs to various ionic strength of mediums should be important in terms of applications. Fig. 3C presents that fluorescent intensity and emission wavelength show neglectable change in solution with a NaCl concentration of 1 M. This good tolerance to high ionic strength should benefit the conjugation of SiQDs with DNA, and in turn improve the stability of fabricated sensing system [32].

The effects of pH value on fluorescent behaviors of SiQDs were estimated. As the pH values changes in the range of 2–11, both fluorescent intensity and peak characteristic of SiQDs remain stable, manifesting the pH-independent characteristic (Fig. 3D). Such a phenomenon is ascribed to the varied surface charge originated from protonation-deprotonation [33]. To be specific, the reason may be ascribed to the overwhelming of hardly ionized groups such as –OH rather than –NH2 or –COOH on SiQDs surface [34]. Noteworthy, pH-independent characteristic is rarely observed in most of the previously reported SiQDs or other types of QDs, and it can endow this SiQDs with the extended application scope [28,35].

The fluorescent intensity of SiQDs was observed to decrease significantly as the temperature raised from 20 to 80 °C with a quenching maximum of 27%, resulting from nonradiative relaxation induced by thermal activation of nonradiative trappings (Fig. 3E) [36]. However, the intensity fully restores after cooling to 20 °C, and such a heating-cooling process can be cycled for 4 times, showing the excellent thermal stability (Fig. 3F). Such a good reversible fluorescent behavior upon the change of temperature is ascribed to the synergistic effect of oxygenous groups and hydrogen bonding with water [37].

Then, such a microwave-assisted synthetic route has the good feasibility to prepare hydrophilic SiQDs. Its merits include high convenience and rapid preparation. Despite the relatively poor fluorescent lifetime and quantum yield, the resultant SiQDs features the high stability, attracting pH-independent characteristic as well as the reversible fluorescent behavior with temperature. And such merits should favor the high analytical performances of the proposed fluorescent sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III fluorescent sensing of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantages of the unique fluorescent behaviors, energy transfer of SiQDs with Rox is explored to achieve the detection of Hg2+. Following the proposed strategy illustrated in Scheme 1B, the feasibility was accessed. Fig. 4 A presents the emission maximum of SiQDs and Rox-DNA at 607 and 414 nm, respectively. The addition of Hg2+ in SiQDs displays no influence on the fluorescent properties. Nevertheless, the mixture of Rox-DNA and SiQDs presents a significantly decreased I607 for Rox-DNA, and the following addition of Hg2+ still shows no influence. On the contrary, the introduction of Exo III in this mixture efficiently restores the fluorescent of Rox. And I414 for SiQDs keeps stable in all tests. Zeta potential test reveals the different surface charge of SiQDs and Rox-DNA, suggesting the formation of their complex via the electrostatic interaction (Fig. 4B).

The detection principle was further investigated with fluorescent lifetime measurements. Fig. 4C reveals the τ of SiQDs keeps constant in different solutions. And the τ of Rox-DNA (τRox-DNA) shows no obvious change upon the addition of Hg2+. However, that value shows a significant decrease in the mixture of SiQDs and Rox-DNA. The introduction of Hg2+ exhibits no influence on it, and then the restored τRox-DNA is observed after the addition of Exo III (Fig. 4D).

Based on the above results, it is reasonable to conclude that the ET between SiQDs and Rox leads to the fluorescent quenching of Rox. For analysis, the addition of Hg2+ induces the formation of double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) by T-Hg2+-T, while the fluorescent of Rox keeps “OFF”. However, the presence of Exo III could cut the formed dsDNA from 3’ end, resulting in the release of Rox and in turn the elimination of the ET effect. Consequently, a fluorescent “turn-on” response mode is observed in terms of Rox, and the fluorescent intensity ratio of SiQDs and Rox, I607/I414, can be used to detect Hg2+.

To achieve better analytical properties, experimental conditions including incubation time of SiQDs and Rox-DNA, amount of Exo III, incubation time of Hg2+, and reaction time of Exo III were optimized in detail (Fig. S2). Then the analytical properties of the proposed SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III sensing platform were estimated under the optimal conditions. Fig. 5 A shows that the value of I607 increases as the concentration of Hg2+ (CHg 2+) rising, while that of I414 keeps stable. Consequently, such platform offers a linear range of 0.02 nM–10 nM with a detection limit of 0.01 nM (Fig. 5B and C). The regression formula is I607/I414 = 0.641Log CHg 2+ + 7.50 (R2 = 0.995).

The characteristic comparison with previous reports is performed (Table 1 ). In terms of detection limit and linear range, SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III sensing platform is among the best fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ analysis [8,13,15,38–43]. Noteworthy, the utilization of ratiometry indeed enhanced the accuracy of analysis, despite the relatively higher detection limit as compared with some single-signal sensors, e.g., DNA-AgNCs/AuNPs/Exo III-based fluorescent sensor [40]. The reason may be associated with the insufficiency in the utilization of sophisticated signal amplification strategy in the present ratiometric fluorescent sensor. And designing novel strategy to acquire enlarged response signal or introducing excellent fluorophore to strengthen the ET with SiQDs may be the efficient way to achieve better analytical properties in the next work.

The selectivity is of great importance for sensing platforms. Here, the selectivity of SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III sensing platform was estimated by measuring the response to the common interferents for Hg2+ analysis, including K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, and Cd2+. As shown in Fig. 5D, neither of these interferents with a concentration of 50 nM displays obvious influence on the value of I607/I414 as compared with that in blank. Only the addition of Hg2+ (0.5 nM) or the mixture of these interferents and Hg2+ causes a significant increase of I607/I414. Thus, the sensing platform has high selectivity toward Hg2+ analysis, due to the specificity to form T-Hg2+-T.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ ions in samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability, SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III sensing platform was applied to the ancient canal water sample and farmland soil sample analysis with the standard addition method. The results listed in Table S1 reveal the high recoveries (95–109%) and low relative standard deviations (1.71–5.23%), implying the satisfactory accuracy. These results are further confirmed by the official method of ICP-MS, demonstrating the comparable reliability of the proposed platform.

We proposed a SiQDs/Rox-DNA/Exo III ratiometric fluorescent assay for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+. A microwave-assisted synthetic route was developed to synthesized the blue emitting SiQDs within 8 min. The resultant SiQDs showed pH-independent spectra and reversible fluorescent response with temperature. Energy transfer between SiQDs and Rox-DNA was utilized to output the ratiometric signal, whereas Exo III acted to trigger and amplify the variations of signal. Such assay offered a broad linear range and a low limit of detection for Hg2+ analysis with comparable reliability of ICP-MS. Our work should provide a promising candidate for the efficient analysis of Hg2+ in various scenarios.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61901193, 22074055), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20200104), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province, Project of Faculty of Agricultural Equipment of Jiangsu University, Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122093.

Wenjia Li: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Dong Liu: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Daming Dong: Writing - review & editing. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing
